If you search for Angular Routing Explained as a Real Application Flow, most explanations start with a simple promise: “a route maps a URL to a component.” That is true, and it’s also the reason many beginner apps become fragile the moment requirements show up.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This usually looks harmless at the beginning. You add a few routes, maybe a guard, and everything feels clean. The code works, but the design is already becoming fragile.
In real projects, routing is not a menu of pages. Routing is the backbone of your application flow: who can enter a screen, what data must exist before it renders, what happens when the data is missing, and how the app behaves when users deep-link, refresh, or open two tabs.
Why routing starts to matter the moment your app is “real”
Tutorial apps reward completion. Real applications reward change.
The first time routing really matters is not when you create /home and /about. It’s when the product manager says:

• “Users should be able to bookmark this exact view.”

• “If you don’t have access, redirect to the upgrade page, but keep the original URL to return later.”

• “If the record was…
